The logo on the copper-colored cylindrical water heater in the image says:  
**Aerobell®**

It is written in a stylized script font, with “Air” and “bell” combined into one word — likely representing air-driven circulation or efficiency. The registered trademark symbol (™) appears after it as well. This was an early brand of electrically heated domestic hot-water storage tanks manufactured by General Electric between 1907–1925 before being discontinued due to patent expiration and competition from other brands like Furr & Company’s standard model heaters starting around 1936.

The aircraft in the image is a **Dutch Air Force F-16AM Fighting Falcon**, specifically tail number **J-062**. It’s parked on an airfield, likely at Volkel or similar Dutch base. The grey livery and national insignia confirm its service with Koninklijke Luchtmacht (Royal Netherlands Air Force). This variant was used for ground attack missions during NATO operations like Operation Allied Force against Yugoslavia — as noted by historical records linking J-062 to that campaign before being retired from front-line duty around 2003–2004 due to maintenance issues but retained for training roles until retirement circa 2015/2017.

It remains preserved today as part of aviation heritage displays worldwide.

The 2016 calendar by Anne Taintor was written and designed by **Anne Taintor**, a well-known American illustrator, designer, and author celebrated for her distinctive retro-style artwork. She is particularly famous for creating the iconic “Sylvia” character — an optimistic woman with red lipstick who appears on many of her calendars and products throughout the years. The text in your image (“you’ll never forget to turn off the oven… if you never turn it on”) reflects one of those witty, nostalgic captions typical of her work. The author and creator behind *Quilt Art 2010 Engagement Calendar* by **Klaudeen Hansen** (also known as Klau) is a well-known quilt designer, educator, and calendar publisher. She has designed numerous quilting-themed calendars for years — including annual editions like “Engagement,” “Anniversary,” “Wedding Anniversary,” etc., often featuring her signature starburst or medallion-style quilters’ art patterns in vibrant colors such as teal, white, black, and gold.

In fact, Klaudeen Hansen created these engagement/anniversary calendars starting around early 2008 (“I Married You” theme), with each year’s edition showcasing new designs based on classic patchwork motifs reimagined through modern color palettes. The 2010 version was part of that long-running series celebrating love milestones via beautiful textile artwork.

So while it may not be published under traditional academic publishing standards, **the artist/designer credited as "author" on the cover is indeed Klaudeen Hansen**.

She also runs [www.klaudie.com](http://www.klauchdie.com/) where you can find more about her work, classes, products, and history of designing these beloved anniversary/calendars since ~2007–2009 era onward.

✅ Final Answer: **Klaudeen Hansen**

The **Y-axis** represents:  
> **“ton of C per toe”**  

This stands for “tons of carbon (C) emitted per tonne-oil equivalent (toe)” — a measure used to quantify how much greenhouse gas emissions result from producing one unit of energy. Lower values indicate cleaner, less carbon-intensive fuels or technologies over time. In this chart, it shows that global primary energy supply has become progressively more efficient and lower-carbon since 1850.

Feathered trees became popular in the U.S. during the **1920s**, imported from Germany and ranging in size from 2 inches to 6 feet — likely as small, decorative items for children or pets (like dogs). This is indicated by their association with Santa Claus toys at Christmas time. 

The timeline also notes that this trend occurred alongside other innovations like glass candleholder light trees (early 1910s) and tree farms expanding rapidly through the same decade due to overharvesting of natural evergreens leading into shortages later on. By 1930, brush companies began manufacturing first artificial bristle trees, marking a shift toward more durable alternatives beyond temporary decorations. So while “feathered” may sound whimsical today, these early versions served both functional purposes within households then plus aesthetic appeal tied closely to holiday festivities back when consumer culture was still developing around new products meant specifically for seasonal use rather than everyday wear-and-tear scenarios outside specific contexts such as parties gatherings etc...
